revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Hayakitsuhiko et Hayakitsuhime]][[ja:ハヤアキツヒコ・ハヤアキツヒメ]][[ko:하야아키츠히코・하야아키츠히메]][[zh:速秋津日子神與速秋津比賣神]]
{{infobox deity|caption=Myths about the birth of the gods (the gods born to Izanagi and Izanami) Display in SVG (compatible browsers only)|image=Descendants of Izanagi and Izanami.svg|children={{ill|Mikumari|simple|User:Immanuelle/mikumari|en:draft|Ame-no-mikumari-no-kami|fr|Mikumari|sv|Mikumari-no-Kami|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} |associated_deities ={{ill|Hayaakitsuhiko|qid=Q116026335|1=Hayaakitsuhiko}}; {{ill|Hayaakitsuhime|qid=Q116026336|1=Hayaakitsuhime}}|equivalent5_type=Japanese |equivalent5={{ill|Shitateruhime|qid=Q11307233|1=Shitateruhime}}}}
Hayakitsuhiko and Hayakitsuhime are [[Kami|deities]] that appear in [[Japanese mythology]]. In the "[[Kojiki]]," they are referred to as Hayakitsuhiko-no-kami and Hayakitsuhime-no-kami, while in the "[[Nihon Shoki]]," they are mentioned as Hayakitsuhi-no-mikoto. The "Kojiki" also refers to them by another name, Minato-no-kami, which means the gods of the port.
== Overview == In the "Kojiki," the deities are named Hayakitsuhiko-no-kami and Hayakitsuhime-no-kami. In the "{{ill|Ōharae no Kotoba|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōharae no Kotoba|qq=}}" (Great Purification Prayer), the name used is Hayakitsuhime. In one volume of the "[[Nihon Shoki]]," they are also known by the alternate name Hayakitsuhi-no-mikoto.
During the {{ill|Kamiumi|lt=creation of gods|fr|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiumi|qq=}}, they were born as a male and female pair from the deities [[Izanagi]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}, with Minato-no-kami being a collective term for them. In the "Nihon Shoki," they are called the gods of water gates and referred to as Hayakitsuhi-no-mikoto.
The "Kojiki" records that the following pairs of deities, all related to water, were born between these two gods:<ref name="Yasumaro" /><ref name=". A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史-2" />
- Ahanagi-no-kami ([[:ja:沫那藝神|沫那藝神]]) and Ahanami-no-kami ([[:ja:沫那美神|沫那美神]])<ref name="Yasumaro" /><ref name=". A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史-2" />
- Tsuranagi-no-kami ([[:ja:頬那藝神|頬那藝神]]) and Tsuranami-no-kami ([[:ja:頬那美神|頬那美神]])<ref name="Yasumaro" /><ref name=". A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史-2" />
- {{ill|Mikumari|lt=Ame-no-Mikumari-no-Kami|simple|User:Immanuelle/mikumari|en:draft|Ame-no-mikumari-no-kami|fr|Mikumari|sv|Mikumari-no-Kami|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Mikumari|lt=Kuni-no-Mikumari-no-Kami|simple|User:Immanuelle/mikumari|en:draft|Ame-no-mikumari-no-kami|fr|Mikumari|sv|Mikumari-no-Kami|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}}<ref name="Yasumaro" /><ref name=". A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史-2" />
- Ame-no-Kuhizamochi-no-kami ([[:ja:天之久比奢母智神|天之久比奢母智神]]) and Kuni-no-Kuhizamochi-no-kami ([[:ja:国之久比奢母智神|国之久比奢母智神]])<ref name="Yasumaro" /><ref name=". A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史-2" />
Additionally, the main deity of the {{ill|8=ja|9=火守神社|Hinomori Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}} in Izumo city, {{ill|8=ja|9=櫛八玉神|Kushiyatama-no-kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシヤタマ}}, who served as a {{ill|kashiwade|ja|%E8%86%B3%E5%A4%AB|lt_k=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kashiwade|qq=}} (ritual server) at the palace built for [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] during the [[Kuni-yuzuri|pacification]] of Ashihara-no-nakatsukuni (the central land of reed plains), is recorded as a grandchild of the two Minato gods.
The word "hayakitsu" in their names is interpreted to mean a person of rapid and robust vigor at a river mouth, with "haya" meaning rapid, "aki" as a borrowed character for "open," and "tsu" interpreted as port.<ref>新潮日本古典集成 古事記</ref>
In the "{{ill|Ōharae no Kotoba|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōharae no Kotoba|qq=}}," it is stated that the sins and impurities washed downstream by the river were swallowed by a deity named Hayakitsuhime (瀬織津姫), referred to in the prayer as sitting among the tides of the eight hundred paths of rough tides.
In the Nihongi, these kami are not named individually but are collectively referred to as Hayaakitsumi, the kami of the River Mouths. The text notes that they were born after the eighth god Yamatsumi.<ref name="Aston">Aston. W.G. (1896) “Nihongi Volume 1: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to AD697”. Tuttle Publishing</ref><ref name=". A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史" />.<ref name="Aston" /><ref name=". A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史-2">{{Cite web|title=Hayaakitsuhime • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/hayaakitsuhime/|access-date=2024-04-12|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB|qq=draft}}</ref>
== Hayaakitsuhiko == Hayaakitsuhiko, whose name translates to "Rushing River Mouth Lad," number nine of the ten deities born to Izanami and Izanagi after they formed the Eightfold Isles and the following six isles, in the account of the Kojiki. He is the god of river estuaries..<ref name="Yasumaro">Yasumaro. O, translated by Gustav Heldt. (2014) “Kojiki. An Account of Ancient Matters”. New York: Columbia University Press</ref><ref name=". A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史">{{Cite web|title=Hayaakitsuhiko • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/hayaakitsuhiko/|access-date=2024-04-12|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>
== Hayaakitsuhime == Hayaakitsuhime, meaning "Lady Rushing River Mouth," the last of the ten deities born to Izanami and Izanagi after they formed the Eightfold Isles and an additional six isles, in the account of the Kojiki. She is the goddess of sea estuaries. She is mentioned in the {{ill|Ōharae no Kotoba|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōharae no Kotoba|qq=}}, Hymn 10, which portrays her as consuming impurities at the sea's converging currents.<ref name="Yasumaro" /><ref name=". A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史-2" />
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Kamiumi|fr|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiumi|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|qid=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese gods|1=List of Japanese deities}}
